text:
Understanding gene regulatory networks requires vast quantities of transcriptomic data to maintain an adequate resolution. Due to this high data requirement, it is commonly the conditions lacking sufficient data that are left uncovered in research, specifically when it comes to rare diseases and/or hard to reach clinical specimens. In the few years, that the concept of transfer learning has been coined, we have seen numerous examples of great successes in the domain of natural language processing and computer vision through the utilization of deep learning frameworks and their pre-trained models, known as off-the-shelf deep learning models, which are initially pre-trained on a large, commonly available dataset that is rich in generic knowledge. Once such a basic, yet generic model is pre-trained and available, it only requires a tiny amount of data for fine tuning it for the specific task that is to be solved.

We introduce a deep learning model named Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based model pre-trained on a dataset of about 30 million single-cell gene expression profiles. Geneformer is capable of producing high-resolution, context-dependent predictions in network biology even when data are scarce. During pre-training, Geneformer learns a fundamental understanding of network behavior, and it learns to encode cellular hierarchy and pairwise gene interactions within its attention map in an unsupervised fashion. This knowledge is then leveraged to discover key regulatory genes and biology of diseases across different contexts.
explanation: The passage uses heavy AI-buzz phrasing and mentions a fictional model name with a large, unspecified dataset, and includes slightly awkward wording that is typical of AI-generated text. This suggests a higher likelihood of AI authorship.
